title: Benchmarking machine learning robustness in Covid-19 genome sequence classification

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## BENCHMARKING MACHINE LEARNING ROBUSTNESS IN COVID-19 SPIKE SEQUENCE CLASSIFICATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The rapid spread of the COVID-19 pandemic has resulted in an unprecedented amount of sequence data of the SARS-CoV-2 viral genome — millions of sequences and counting. This amount of data, while being orders of magnitude beyond the capacity of traditional approaches to understanding the diversity, dynamics and evolution of viruses, is nonetheless a rich resource for machine learning (ML) and deep learning (DL) approaches as alternatives for extracting such important information from these data. It is of hence utmost importance to design a framework for testing and benchmarking the robustness of these ML and DL approaches.

This paper the first (to our knowledge) to explore such a framework. In this paper, we introduce several ways to perturb SARS-CoV-2 spike protein sequences in ways that mimic the error profiles of common sequencing platforms such as Illumina and PacBio. We show from experiments on a wide array of ML approaches from naive Bayes to logistic regression, that DL approaches are more robust (and accurate) to such adverarial attacks to the input sequences, while _k_ -mer based feature vector representations are more robust than the baseline one-hot embedding. Our benchmarking framework may developers of futher ML and DL techniques to properly assess their approaches towards understanding the behaviour of the SARS-CoV-2 virus, or towards avoiding possible future pandemics.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A novel (RNA) coronavirus was identified in January 2020, which began the COVID-19 pandemic that is still ongoing today. With the help of sequencing technology and phylogenetic analysis, the scientific community disclosed that this novel coronavirus has 50% similarity with the Middle-Eastern Repiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (MERS-CoV), 79% sequencing similarity to Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (SARS-CoV) — also known simply as “SARS” — and more than 85% similarity with coronavirusus found in bats. Further studies confirmed that bats are the likely reservoir of these coronaviruses; however, the ecological separation of bats from humans indicates that some other organisms may have acted as intermediate hosts. Considering all scientific evidence, the International Committee on Taxonomy of Viruses named the novel RNA virus SARS-CoV-2 (Wu et al., 2020; Park, 2020; Zhang & Holmes, 2020).

RNA viruses generally introduce errors during replication, the resulting mutations incorporated into the viral genome after repeated replication within a single host, generating a heterogenous population of viral quasispecies. However, SARS-CoV-2 has an excellent proofreading mechanism that encodes a nonstructural protein 14 (nsp14) allowing it to have a 10-fold lower mutation rate than the typical RNA viruses. Epidemiologists estimate that SARS-CoV-2 has 33 genomic mutations per year on average. Some of these mutations are advantageous, leading to the more infections variants of SARS-CoV-2 that continue to emerge (Nelson, 2021). Because of this relatively slow process of accumlating mutations, and the fact that each major variant can be characterized or differentiated by a handfull of mutations (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021), small perturbations (errors) in the sequence should not lead to mistaking one variant from another. Moreover, most of these changes occur in the S gene — the segment of the genome which encodes the surface, or spike, protein (see Figure 1) — hence, characterizing variants using (the resulting tran-

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

scribed) spike proteins is sufficient for the classification task (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifications and Definitions, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020).

Figure 1: The SARS-CoV-2 genome codes for several proteins, including the surface, or spike protein. Sequence of spike protein is important to understand various molecular mechanisms of SARS-Cov-2. While incorporation of error in spike sequences can bias the final result (Huang et al., 2020).

The diminishing cost of next-generation sequencing (NGS) technology has aided scientists from different parts of the world to generate SARS-CoV-2 whole-genome sequencing (WGS). The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) of the United States has also provided a wealth of information on resources, tools, and protocols for SARS-CoV-2 WGS from different sequencing platforms such as Illumina, PacBio, and Ion Torrent. Finally, the Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data (GISAID) hosts the largest SARS-CoV-2 genome sequencing dataset to date — the largest of any virus in history, with millions of sequences. This unprecedented amount of genomic data generation and easy availability allowed researchers to explore the molecular mechanism, genetic variability, evolutionary progress, and capability of development and spread of novel variants of the virus. On the other hand, this amount of data exceeds the capacity of methods such as Nextstrain (Hadfield et al., 2018) or even the more recent IQTREE2 (Minh et al., 2020) by several orders of magnitude — a Big Data challenge. As a result, recent alternative approaches based on clustering and classification of sequences, _e.g._ , to identify major variants, have appeared in the literature (Melnyk et al., 2020; Ali et al., 2021a;b; Ali & Patterson, 2021), with promising accuracy and scalability properties.

Many issues still remain, however, such as sequencing errors being mistaken for mutations in different analyses, when studying the evolutionary and transmission pattern of the SARS-CoV-2 (GISAID History, 2021; Arons et al., 2020), or other viruses. Incorporation of error in NGS sequences due to contamination in sample preparation, sequencing technology, or genome assembly methodology are other confounding factors. Generally, computational biologists filter those sequences having errors or mask those sequence fragments having errors. For example, each GISAID (GISAID Website, 2021) sequence is a consensus sequence from the intra-host viral population sampled from the patient, averaging out the minor variations which exist in this population. While such a consensus sequence is a good representative of this population, _i.e._ , it is still precise enough to capture the SARS-CoV-2 variant harboured by the infected individual, it comes at the cost of losing this important information, such as these minor variations. Such minor variations, when given enough time to evolve, _e.g._ , within an immunocompromized individual, can become dominant — one of the theories theory behind the emergence of the Alpha variant (Frampton et al., 2021).

What this means is that many of machine learning approaches towards clustering and classification of sequences (Ali et al., 2021a;b; Ali & Patterson, 2021) have been operating under rather idealized conditions of virtually error-free consensus sequences. Moveover, these methods rely on a _k_ -mer based feature vector representation — an approach that does not even rely on alignment of the sequences, something which can also introduce bias (Golubchik et al., 2007). Such a framework should easily cope with errors as well — something machine learning approaches can do very

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

naturally (Du et al., 2021). There is hence a great need for some way to reliably benchmark such methods for robustness to errors, which is what we carry out in this paper.

We highlight the main contributions of this paper as follows:

- We propose several ways of introducing errors into spike sequences which reflect the error profiles of modern NGS technologies such as Illumina and PacBio;
- We demonstrate that the _k_ -mer based approach is more robust to such errors when compared to the baseline (one-hot encoding); and
- We show that deep learning is generally more robust in handling these errors than machine learning models.

Moreover, we extend our error testing procedure as a framework for bechmarking the performance of different ML methods in terms of classification accuracy and robustness to different types of simulated random errors in the sequences. The two types of errors that we introduce are “consecutive” and “random” errors (see Sec. 3.4). Random errors are just point mutations, which happen uniformly at random along the protein sequence, simulating closely the behaviour of Illumina sequenceing technolgies (Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021). Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-reads technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing (Dohm et al., 2020).

This paper is structured as follows. In Sec. 2 we discuss related work. In Sec. 3 we discuss some approaches we benchmark, and then how we benchmark: the type of adversarial attacks we use. Sce. 4 details the experiments, and Sec. 5 gives the results. Finally, we conclude this paper in Sec. 6.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Assessing and benchmarking the robustness of ML or DL approaches by a series of adversarial attacks is popular in the image classification domain (Hendrycks & Dietterich, 2019), but there are others that are closer to the domain of molecular data. In (Schwalbe-Koda et al., 2021), the authors provide a series of realistic adversarial attacks to benchmark methods which predict chemical properties from atomistic simulations _e.g._ , molecular conformation, reactions and phase transitions. Even closer to the subject of our paper — protein sequences – the authors of (Jha et al., 2021) show that methods, such as AlphaFold (Jumper et al., 2021) and RoseTTAFold (Baek et al., 2021) which employ deep neural networks to predict protein conformation are not robust: producing drastically different protein structures as a result of very small biologically meaningful perturbations in the protein sequence. Our approach is similar, albeit with the different goal of classification: namely, to explore how a small number of point mutations (simulating the error introduced certain types of NGS technologies) can affect the downstream classification of different machine learning and deep learning approaches. After getting the numerical representation, a popular approach is to get the kernel matrix and give that matrix as input to traditional machine learning classifiers like support vector machines (SVM) (Leslie et al., 2003; Farhan et al., 2017; Kuksa et al., 2012). However, these methods are expensive in terms of space complexity. Authors in (Ali & Patterson, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020) proposes an efficient embedding method for classification and clustering of spike sequences. However, their approaches are either not scalable or perform poorly on bigger datasets.

## 3 PROPOSED APPROACH

In this section, we start by explaining the baseline model for spike sequence classification. After that, we will explain our deep learning model in detail.

## 3.1 ONE-HOT ENCODING (OHE) BASED EMBEDDING

Authors in (Kuzmin et al., 2020) propose that classification of viral hosts of the coronavirus can be done by using spike sequences only. For this purpose, a fixed-length one-hot encoding based feature vector is generated for the spike sequences. In spike sequence, we have 21 unique characters (amino acids) that are _“ACDEFGHIKLMNPQRSTVWXY”_ . Also, note that the length of each spike

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

sequence is 1273 with the stopping character ‘*’ at the 1274 _[th]_ position. When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be 21 _×_ 1273 = 26733. This high dimensionality could create the problem of “Curse of Dimensionality (CoD)”. To solve CoD problem, any dimensionality reduction method can be used such as Principal Component Analysis (Abdi & Williams, 2010). After reducing the dimensions of the feature vectors, classical Machine Learning (ML) algorithms can be applied to classify the spike sequences. One major problem with such OHE based representation is that it does not preserve the order of the amino acids very efficiently (Ali et al., 2021a). If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors. Since the order of amino acids is important in case of sequential data, OHE fails to give us efficient results (Ali et al., 2021a). In this paper, we use OHE as baseline embedding method.

## 3.2 K-MERS BASED REPRESENTATION

A popular approach to preserve the ordering of the sequential information is to take the sliding window based substrings (called mers) of length _k_ . This _k_ -mers based representation is recently proven to be useful in classifying the spike sequences effectively (Ali et al., 2021a) (see Figure 2 for example of k-mers). In this approach, first, the k-mers of length _k_ are computed for each spike

Figure 2: Example of different length _k_ -mers in a spike sequence “MDPEG”.

sequence. Then a fixed length frequency vector is generated corresponding to each spike sequence, which contains the count of each k-mer in that sequence. One advantage of using k-mers based approach is that it is an _“alignment free”_ method unlike OHE, which requires the sequences to be aligned to the reference genome.

**Remark 1** _Sequence alignment is an expensive process and requires reference sequence (genome) (Chowdhury & Garai, 2017; Denti et al., 2021). It may also introduce bias into the result (Golubchik et al., 2007)._

The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are:

where _N_ is the length of the sequence. The variable _k_ is the user defined parameter. In this paper, we take _k_ = 3 (decided empirically). Since we have 1273 length spike sequences, the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is 1273 _−_ 3 + 1 = 1271.

## 3.2.1 FREQUENCY VECTOR GENERATION

After generating the k-mers, the next step is to generate the fixed-length numerical representation (frequency vector) for the set of k-mers in a spike sequence. Let the set of amino acids in the whole dataset is represented by alphabet Σ. Now, length of the frequency vector will be _|_ Σ _|[k]_ (all possible combinations of k-mers in Σ of length _k_ ). Recall that in our dataset, we have 21 unique amino acids in any spike sequence. Therefore, the length of frequency vector in our case would be 21[3] = 9261 (when we take _k_ = 3).

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Note that CoD could be a problem in case of k-mers based numerical representation of the spike sequence. To deal with this problem, authors in (Ali & Patterson, 2021) use an approximate kernel method that map such vectors into low dimensional euclidean space using an approach, called Random Fourier Features (RFF) (Rahimi et al., 2007). Unlike kernel trick, which compute the inner product between the lifted data points _φ_ (i.e. _⟨φ_ ( _a_ ) _, φ_ ( _b_ ) _⟩_ = _f_ ( _a, b_ ), where _a, b ∈R[d]_ and f(a,b) is any positive definite function), RFF maps the data into low dimensional euclidean inner product space. More formally:

RFF tries to approximate the inner product between any pair of transformed points.

where _z_ is low dimensional representation. Since _z_ is the approximate representation, we can use it as an input for the classical ML models and analyse their behavior (as done in Spike2Vec (Ali & Patterson, 2021)). However, we show that such approach performs poorly on larger size datasets (hence poor scalability).

## 3.3 KERAS CLASSIFIER

We use a deep learning-based model called the Keras Classification model (also called Keras classifier) to further improve the performance that we got from Spike2Vec. For keras classifier, we use a sequential constructor. It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. 9261). We use ”rectifier” activation function for this classifier. Moreover, we use “softmax” activation function in the output layer. At last, we use the efficient Adam gradient descent optimization algorithm with “sparse categorical crossentropy” loss function (used for multi-class classification problem). It computes the crossentropy loss between the labels and predictions. The batch size and number of epochs are taken as 100 and 10, respectively for training the model. For the input to this keras classifier, we separately use OHE and _k_ -mers based frequency vectors.
**Remark 2** _Note that we are using “sparse categorical crossentropy” rather than simple “categorical crossentropy” because we are using integer labels rather than one-hot representation of labels._

## 3.4 ADVERSARIAL EXAMPLES CREATION

We use two types of approaches to generate adversarial examples so that we can test the robustness of our proposed model. These approaches are “Random Error generation” and “Consecutive Error generation”.

In random error generation, we randomly select a fraction of spike sequences (we call them the set of errored sequences for reference) from the test set (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%). For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The ideas is that this simulates the errors made by NGS technologies such as Illumina (Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021).

In consecutive error generation, the first step is the same as in random error generation (getting random set of spike sequences from the test set “set of errored sequences”). For this set of errored sequences, rather than randomly flipping a specific percentage of amino acid’s values for each spike sequence (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%), we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence. More formally, it is a consecutive set of amino acids (at random position) in the spike sequence for which we flip the values. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequencly found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio (Dohm et al., 2020).

Using the two approaches to generate adversarial examples, we generate a new test set and evaluate the performance of the ML and deep learning models. To measure the performance, we also apply two different strategies. One strategy is called Accuracy and the other is called robustness. In the case of the Accuracy, we compute the average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for the whole test set including adversarial and non-adversarial examples.

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples (set of errored spike sequences) rather than considering the whole test set and compute average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for them.

## 4 EXPERIMENTAL SETUP

All experiments are conducted using an Intel(R) Xeon(R) CPU E7-4850 v4 @ 2 _._ 10GHz having Ubuntu 64 bit OS (16 _._ 04 _._ 7 LTS Xenial Xerus) with 3023 GB memory. Our pre-processed data is also available online[1] , which can be used after agreeing to terms and conditions of GISAID[2] . For the classification algorithms, we use 1% data for training and 99% for testing. Note that our data split and pre-processing follow those of (Ali & Patterson, 2021).

## 4.1 DATASET STATISTICS

We used the (aligned) spike protein from a popular and publicly available database of SARS-CoV-2 sequences, GISAID. In our dataset, we have 2 _,_ 519 _,_ 386 spike sequences along with the COVID-19 variant information. The total number of unique variants in our dataset are 1327. Since not all variants have significant representation in our data, we only select the variants having more than 10 _,_ 000 sequences. After this preprocessing, we are left with 1 _,_ 995 _,_ 195 spike sequences. The dataset statistics for the prepossessed data are given in Table 1. Since most of the variants are new, we do not have all the information available for all them. Therefore, we put “-” in the field in Table 1 for which we do not have any information available online.

To visualise if there is any (natural) clustering in our data, we generated 2d representation of the k-mers based frequency vectors using t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) approach (Van der M. & Hinton, 2008). The t-SNE plot for some of the popular variants is given in Figure 3. Note that we have not given the t-SNE plot for all variants. This is because t-SNE method is computationally very expensive (runtime is _O_ ( _N_[2] ), where _N_ is the number of data-points (Pezzotti et al., 2016)) and take a lot of time on _≈_ 1 _._ 9 million sequences.

Figure 3: t-SNE plot for the k-mers based feature vectors of spike sequences.

## 5 RESULTS AND DISCUSSION

In this section, we first show the comparison of our deep learning model with the baselines. We then show the results for the two approaches for adversarial examples generation and compare different ML and DL methods. Overall, we elucidate our key findings in the following subsections.

## 5.1 EFFECTIVENESS OF DEEP LEARNING

Table 2 contains the (accuracy) results for our keras classifier and its comparison with different ML models namely Naive Nayes (NB), Logistic Regression (LR), and Ridge Classifier (RC). For keras classifier, we use both OHE and k-mers based embedding approaches separately. We can observe from the results that keras classifier with k-mers based frequency vectors is by far the best approach as compared to the other baselines.
Table 2: Variants Classification Results (1% training set and 99% testing set) for top 22 variants (1995195 spike sequences).

To test the robustness of these ML and DL methods, we use both “consecutive error generation” and “random error generation” separately. Table 3 shows the (accuracy) results (using keras classifier with k-mers because that was the best model from Table 2) for the consecutive error generation

(b) Accuracy (x axis) vs Robustness (y axis) plot (for average accuracy values) for different ML and DL methods for 10% adversarial sequences from the test set with 10% amino acids flips.

The accuracy and the robustness results for the other ML models (using Spike2Vec approach) are given in Tables 7 and Table 8, respectively in the appendix. From the results, we can conclude that our deep learning based model is more accurate and robust than other compared machine learning models. Another interesting outcome from the results is that k-mers based feature vector is more robust that traditional one-hot embedding. This is a kind of “proof of concept” that since k-mers preserve the order of the amino acids in a spike sequence (as order matters in genomic sequence data), it outperforms the traditional OHE by a significant margin.

The accuracy vs robustness comparison (for average accuracy values) of different ML and DL methods for 10% adversarial sequences from the test set with 10% amino acids flips is given in Figure 4b. We can see that keras classifier performs best as compared to the other ML methods. This shows that not only our DL method show better predictive performance, but is also more robust as compared to the other ML models.

## 6 CONCLUSION

One interesting future extension is using other alignment free methods such as Minimizers, which have been successful in representing metagenomics data. Since an intra-host viral population can be viewed as a metagenomics sample, this could be appropriate in this context. Another future direction is introducing more adversarial attacks resembling, in more detail, the error profiles of specific sequencing technologies. One could even fine-tune this to the particular experimental setting in which one obtained a sample, similar to sequencing reads simulators such as PBSIM (for PacBio reads).

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses a timely and relevant problem — benchmarking ML robustness in SARS-CoV-2 spike sequence classification — and proposes a framework using biologically motivated perturbations. It correctly identifies limitations of one-hot encoding and highlights benefits of k-mer representations, aligning with current practices in genomics. The experimental setup includes realistic error models from NGS platforms, which strengthens its practical relevance.

Overview issues:

- Temporal data leakage undermines evolutionary robustness claims: The dataset includes sequences spanning multiple years (e.g., Alpha, Delta) but the train-test split uses random 1% training and 99% testing without temporal stratification. This risks data leakage, as later variants may share mutations with earlier ones, and models could exploit temporal signal rather than learn biologically meaningful features. Given the rapid evolution of SARS-CoV-2, a temporally disjoint split — training on early variants and testing on later ones — is essential to assess generalization to novel variants. Without this, the claimed robustness may reflect memorization of chronological patterns rather than true biological generalization. The paper must re-evaluate performance using a time-based split to support its conclusions about model reliability in real-world deployment.

- Adversarial perturbations lack biological plausibility: The paper simulates sequencing errors via random amino acid flips and consecutive indels, but does not constrain these changes to reflect actual mutational processes or protein stability constraints. Real viral evolution follows paths governed by fitness landscapes; arbitrary flips may generate non-viable sequences that never appear in nature. Moreover, the model is trained on consensus sequences but tested on heavily corrupted versions that do not mimic real quasispecies diversity. A more meaningful evaluation would use biologically constrained perturbations — such as single-nucleotide variants mapped through codon translation, or insertions/deletions observed in real long-read data — to ensure adversarial examples are evolutionarily plausible. This gap weakens the applicability of the robustness findings to real-world scenarios.

- Overstatement of deep learning superiority without architectural justification: The paper claims deep learning models are more robust than classical ML, based solely on a single Keras classifier with one hidden layer. However, no ablation study varies depth, width, or architecture type (e.g., CNNs, Transformers), nor compares against modern DNA language models like DNABERT-2 or Nucleotide Transformer, which are standard in genomic sequence analysis. The chosen model lacks architectural novelty or biological interpretability, and the training runtime (over 13,000 seconds) suggests inefficiency. Without benchmarking against established baselines or exploring architectural space, the conclusion that 'DL is more robust' is premature. The authors should either temper their claims or expand the model comparison to include state-of-the-art architectures.

- k-mer frequency vector dimensionality introduces instability: The k-mer representation uses a fixed vocabulary of size $21^3 = 9261$, assuming all possible 3-mers are known at training time. However, in a rapidly evolving virus, new k-mers can emerge in test variants not seen during training, leading to out-of-vocabulary issues. The paper does not address how unseen k-mers are handled — ignored, mapped to unknown tokens, or otherwise — nor does it evaluate performance degradation under vocabulary shift. This omission is critical, as real-world deployment requires generalization to novel sequences. The authors should report performance on held-out variant lineages or simulate vocabulary drift to assess robustness to evolutionary novelty, rather than just random corruption.

- Insufficient detail on adversarial generation protocol: The method for generating 'random' and 'consecutive' errors lacks key specifications: whether flipped amino acids respect biochemical constraints (e.g., polarity, charge), how positions are selected (uniformly or biased toward variable regions), and whether stop codons or frameshifts are allowed. These choices affect the realism of the perturbation. For example, flipping a conserved cysteine in the receptor-binding domain is more disruptive than changing a surface loop residue. Without documenting the exact procedure and constraints, the adversarial test is not reproducible or biologically grounded. The authors must provide a complete algorithmic description, including any biological filters applied during error injection.

- Misleading interpretation of robustness metrics: The paper defines 'robustness' as accuracy on only the corrupted subset of test sequences. However, Tables 4 and 6 show drastic drops in performance — e.g., accuracy falling to 6% at high perturbation levels — which contradicts the narrative of strong robustness. The authors highlight isolated cases where performance remains high but ignore overall degradation trends. Furthermore, F1-macro scores near zero indicate complete failure on minority classes. This suggests the model is brittle under stress, not robust. The interpretation should acknowledge these limitations and avoid overclaiming; alternatively, the perturbation levels could be reduced to reflect realistic error rates (typically <1%) rather than extreme 20% corruption.

- No demonstration on biologically realistic mutation paths: The paper claims its error models simulate Illumina and PacBio error profiles, but does not verify that the perturbations align with actual mutational processes in SARS-CoV-2. Readers will wonder whether arbitrary amino acid flips produce sequences that are evolutionarily viable or even biochemically plausible. To strengthen the claims, the authors should simulate mutations at the nucleotide level using known codon transition probabilities and map them to amino acid changes, then re-run the benchmark. For example, they could use a standard model like HKY + Γ to generate single-nucleotide variants, translate them through the spike gene’s coding sequence, and assess classifier performance under such constrained perturbations. This would ground the robustness evaluation in biological reality rather than synthetic noise.

- Missing ablation study for k-mer length and model capacity: The paper fixes k=3 for k-mers and uses a single-layer Keras classifier without justifying these choices. A natural question is whether results hold for other k values or more expressive models. The authors should include an ablation study varying k from 2 to 5 and report performance trends, especially since longer k-mers may capture more biological signal but increase sparsity. Similarly, they should test a shallow CNN or MLP with multiple layers to isolate the effect of model depth. Without this, it's unclear if the observed robustness stems from the k-mer representation or simply from using any reasonable feature extractor with sufficient capacity. Adding this analysis would clarify the relative importance of architecture versus encoding.

- Unvalidated assumption that robustness generalizes across variants: The paper evaluates robustness on a mixed test set but does not assess whether performance degrades on emerging or minority variants. Given that new lineages (e.g., Omicron) can have dozens of spike mutations, the current evaluation may overstate generalization. The authors should re-run their analysis by training on early variants (e.g., Alpha, Beta) and testing on later ones (e.g., Delta, Omicron), injecting errors only in the holdout variants. This would test whether the k-mer + DL pipeline remains robust to both evolutionary divergence and technical noise. Such a design is standard in genomic forecasting and necessary to support claims about real-world applicability.

- No discussion of feasibility at scale: The paper claims its framework can guide future ML development but omits practical considerations for large-scale deployment. With nearly 2 million sequences, the k-mer frequency vector (length 9261) and training runtime (over 13,000 seconds) raise scalability concerns. The authors should discuss how their method would handle real-time classification as new sequences arrive, including memory use, update frequency, and computational bottlenecks. A simple addition would be estimating the cost per sequence and comparing it to streaming methods like MinHash or CountSketch, which the authors mention in passing. Without this, the framework risks appearing theoretical rather than actionable.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Re-evaluate all models using a temporally disjoint train-test split (e.g., train on pre-2021 data, test on 2021+ variants) to assess evolutionary robustness.

- Revise the adversarial perturbation method to use biologically plausible mutations (e.g., nucleotide-level SNVs mapped to amino acids, observed indel patterns) and document all generation parameters.

- Include comparisons with modern genomic foundation models (e.g., DNABERT-2, Nucleotide Transformer) or at least justify their absence.

- Clarify how out-of-vocabulary k-mers are handled and evaluate performance under vocabulary shift.

- Correct the interpretation of robustness results to reflect actual performance degradation at high error levels, rather than selective reporting of favorable cases.

comments.title: Consecutive error model misrepresents indels
comments.quote: In consecutive error generation, the first step is the same as in random error generation (getting random set of spike sequences from the test set “set of errored sequences”). For this set of errored sequences, rather than randomly flipping a specific percentage of amino acid’s values for each spike sequence (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%), we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence. More formally, it is a consecutive set of amino acids (at random position) in the spike sequence for which we flip the values. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The idea is that this simulates indel errors, which are frequency found in third generation long-read technologies such as PacBio *(Dohm et al., 2020)*.
comments.explanation: The method described replaces a block of consecutive amino acids with other amino acids, which constitutes a substitutional artifact, not an indel. True indels involve insertions or deletions that alter sequence length and potentially reading frame—hallmarks of PacBio errors. By preserving length and only changing residue identities, this perturbation fails to simulate actual indel noise. To align with the claimed motivation, the authors should either introduce actual insertions or deletions at the nucleotide level or reframe the perturbation as a clustered substitution, not an indel.

comments.title: Random amino acid flips lack biochemical constraints
comments.quote: For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly. At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The ideas is that this simulates the errors made by NGS technologies such as Illumina *(Stoler & Nekrutenko, 2021)*.
comments.explanation: Illumina errors occur at the nucleotide level and are constrained by the genetic code: most substitutions are transitions (e.g., A→G) and map to conservative amino acid changes due to codon degeneracy. Randomly flipping amino acids ignores these biological constraints and can generate implausible changes (e.g., hydrophobic to charged). This produces sequences that are not representative of real sequencing errors. To improve realism, perturbations should be introduced at the nucleotide level using empirically observed error profiles and translated through the spike gene’s coding sequence.

comments.title: Robustness metric conflates accuracy on corrupted subset
comments.quote: For our second strategy (robustness), we only consider the adversarial examples (set of errored spike sequences) rather than considering the whole test set and compute average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for them.
comments.explanation: Defining robustness as accuracy on corrupted sequences alone ignores performance degradation relative to clean data. A model that drops from 87% to 60% accuracy under perturbation may still show 60% in the 'robustness' metric, masking significant vulnerability. True robustness should reflect stability—e.g., via relative accuracy (adversarial / clean) or area under a perturbation curve. Without such a comparative measure, the term 'robustness' is misleading and allows poor stability to be framed as acceptable performance.

comments.title: Contradictory accuracy trends in Table 4 undermine narrative
comments.quote: We can observe that keras classifier is able to perform efficiently even with higher proportion of error.
comments.explanation: This claim contradicts the data: in Table 4, accuracy drops to 0.15 (15%) when 5% of sequences have 10% error, and to 0.06 (6%) under higher perturbation. F1 macro scores fall below 0.05, indicating near-total failure on minority classes. These are not signs of efficient performance. The narrative selectively highlights isolated cases (e.g., 60% accuracy) while ignoring systematic collapse under moderate stress. The authors must revise their interpretation to acknowledge that the model is highly sensitive to error concentration, especially when both the fraction of corrupted sequences and per-sequence error rate increase.

comments.title: F1 macro near zero invalidates robustness claim
comments.quote: From the results, we can conclude that our deep learning based model is more accurate and robust than other compared machine learning models.
comments.explanation: F1 macro scores in Table 4 fall to 0.01–0.03 under many conditions, indicating the model fails to classify minority variants—precisely those most likely to be emerging or clinically significant. In imbalanced classification, high overall accuracy can coexist with complete neglect of rare classes. The conclusion of 'robustness' is therefore unjustified: robustness in variant classification should require consistent performance across all lineages, not just dominance by majority classes. The authors must qualify their claim or provide evidence of balanced class recovery under perturbation.

comments.title: Incorrect conflation of error types with sequencing technologies
comments.quote: Random errors are just point mutations, which happen uniformly at random along the protein sequence, simulating closely the behaviour of Illumina sequenceing technolgies *(Stoler and Nekrutenko, 2021)*. Consecutive errors, on the other hand, are small subsequences of consective errors, which can model insertion-delection (indel) errors which are common in third generation long-reads technologies such as Pacific Biosciences (PacBio) SMRT sequencing *(Dohm et al., 2020)*.
comments.explanation: Illumina errors are not uniform amino acid 'point mutations'—they are nucleotide-level substitutions with context-dependent biases (e.g., higher in homopolymers). PacBio errors are stochastic single-base indels distributed across reads, not clustered blocks. The paper’s simulations misrepresent both platforms: random amino acid flips do not reflect Illumina’s substitution profile, and consecutive substitutions do not mimic PacBio’s indel pattern. These simplifications weaken the biological relevance of the benchmark. The authors should either refine the perturbation models or clarify that they are approximate proxies, not faithful simulations.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about scalability of prior methods
comments.quote: Authors in *(Ali and Patterson, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020)* proposes an efficient embedding method for classification and clustering of spike sequences. However, their approaches are either not scalable or perform poorly on bigger datasets.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that prior methods 'perform poorly on bigger datasets' and 'are not scalable' but provides no empirical comparison or citation to support this. In fact, Ali & Patterson (2021) explicitly design for scalability using k-mer embeddings on large SARS-CoV-2 datasets. Without benchmarking against these works or citing external validation, this claim risks misrepresenting prior contributions. The authors should either provide evidence or rephrase to acknowledge the design goals of the cited methods without asserting performance deficiencies.

comments.title: Failure to address k-mer out-of-vocabulary issues
comments.quote: Then a fixed length frequency vector is generated corresponding to each spike sequence, which contains the count of each k-mer in that sequence.
comments.explanation: The method assumes a fixed vocabulary of $21^3 = 9261$ 3-mers, but does not specify how novel k-mers in test sequences (e.g., from new variants) are handled. In rapidly evolving viruses, unseen k-mers are expected, and ignoring them or setting counts to zero could degrade performance. This is a critical gap for a paper claiming robustness. The authors must clarify whether the vocabulary is fixed at training time and how OOV k-mers are treated—e.g., via zero-padding, hashing, or smoothing—to ensure reproducibility and assess generalization to evolutionary novelty.

comments.title: No justification for k=3 choice despite empirical claim
comments.quote: In this paper, we take  $k = 3$  (decided empirically).
comments.explanation:
The authors state $k=3$ was 'decided empirically' but present no ablation study, no performance comparison across $k 
eq 3$, and no discussion of trade-offs (e.g., sparsity vs. motif capture). For a methodological paper emphasizing benchmarking, this omission undermines credibility. The choice may be biologically motivated (codon-level), but the claim of empirical selection demands supporting evidence. Add results from a $k 	imes$ model comparison or retract the 'empirically' justification.

comments.title: Hidden layer size mischaracterized as equal to input
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. $9261$).
comments.explanation: A hidden layer with 9261 units fed by a 9261-dimensional input implies ~85 million parameters—extremely large for a 1% training split and prone to overfitting. More critically, this architecture cannot accept both k-mer (9261-D) and OHE (~26,733-D) inputs without reconfiguration. The claim that both are used with the same model is therefore inconsistent. The authors must clarify whether two separate models were trained or correct the architectural description to reflect actual input dimensions.

comments.title: Incorrect vector length calculation
comments.quote: on. When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be  $21 \times 1273 = 26733$ . This hi
comments.explanation: The text states the sequence has 1273 amino acids plus a stop character at position 1274, but the OHE calculation uses only 21 symbols over 1273 positions. If the stop character ‘*’ is included and treated as a 22nd symbol, the vector length should be $22 \times 1274 = 28028$. If it is excluded, the sequence length is 1273. The current mix of assumptions is inconsistent. Revise to clarify whether the stop codon is encoded and adjust the dimensionality accordingly.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of OHE sensitivity to order
comments.quote: If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors.
comments.explanation: This is incorrect: swapping amino acids at different positions changes the OHE vector because each position is encoded independently. The Euclidean distance *does* change under such swaps. The authors appear to confuse raw OHE with bag-of-words representations. While downstream pooling may discard positional information, the OHE vector itself is sensitive to order. Clarify that the concern applies only if positional structure is lost during aggregation, not in the encoding itself.

comments.title: Inconsistent robustness definition invalidates comparison
comments.quote: The robustness results for the consecutive error generation method are given in Table 4. Although we cannot see any clear pattern in this case, the keras classifier is giving us comparatively higher performance in some of the settings.
comments.explanation: The paper uses 'robustness' to refer to accuracy on corrupted sequences, but Figure 4b plots 'accuracy vs robustness' as if they are distinct metrics. This conflation suggests robustness is a derived quantity, but no such definition is provided. Without a consistent, quantitative definition—e.g., relative accuracy drop or area under perturbation curve—the comparison across models lacks rigor. The claim that 'DL is more robust' rests on this ambiguity. Define robustness formally and compute it uniformly across models.

comments.title: Duplicate table label creates ambiguity
comments.quote:
Table 4: Robustness (only error seq. in test set) results (consecutive error seq.) for Keras Classifier with k-mers and different % of errors.

|  % of Seq. | % of Error in each Seq. | Acc. | Prec. | Recall | F1 (weighted) | F1 (Macro) | ROC-AUC | Train. runtime (sec.)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  5 % | 5 % | 0.60 | 0.57 | 0.60 | 0.54 | 0.10 | 0.55 | 13882.03  |
comments.explanation: Two distinct tables are labeled 'Table 4'. The first presents accuracy on the full test set (matching Table 3's description), while the second contains results on corrupted sequences. The text refers to the second as showing 'robustness (only error seq.)', but the duplication creates confusion about which results support which claims. This risks misattribution of findings. Remove the first 'Table 4' label and renumber to ensure each table has a unique identifier.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about k-mers preserving order
comments.quote: Another interesting outcome from the results is that k-mers based feature vector is more robust that traditional one-hot embedding. This is a kind of "proof of concept" that since k-mers preserve the order of the amino acids in a spike sequence (as order matters in genomic sequence data), it outperforms the traditional OHE by a significant margin.
comments.explanation: Both OHE and k-mer representations preserve sequence order: OHE via positional encoding, k-mers via local substring context. The k-mer frequency vector, however, aggregates counts and discards absolute position—unlike OHE, which retains it. The performance advantage likely stems from motif sensitivity or dimensionality, not superior order preservation. The argument misrepresents both methods. Attribute the gain to local pattern detection, not order, which both encode in different ways.

comments.title: Inconsistent variant labeling in dataset table
comments.quote:
|  Pango Lineage | Region | Labels | # of Mu-tations S-gene/Genome | Num. of se-quences  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  B.1.1.7 | UK (Galloway et al., 2021) | Alpha | 8/17 | 976077  |
|  B.1.351 | South Africa (Galloway et al., 2021) | Beta | 9/21 | 20829  |
|  B.1.617.2 | India (Yadav et al., 2021) | Delta | 8/17 | 242820  |
|  P.1 | Brazil (Naveca et al., 2021) | Gamma | 10/21 | 56948  |
|  B.1.427 | California (Zhang et al., 2021) | Epsilon | 3/5 | 17799  |
|  AY.4 | India (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifica-tions and Definitions, 2021) | Delta | - | 156038  |
comments.explanation: B.1.617.2 and AY.4 are both labeled 'Delta', but AY.4 is a sublineage of B.1.617.2. Treating them as separate rows with the same label risks class leakage or misrepresentation unless explicitly collapsed. The table does not clarify whether these are distinct classes or merged categories. This ambiguity affects reproducibility and interpretation. Specify whether sublineages are grouped under parent labels and, if so, document the mapping.

comments.title: Train-test split contradicts cited source
comments.quote: For the classification algorithms, we use  $1\%$  data for training and  $99\%$  for testing. Note that our data split and pre-processing follow those of (Ali & Patterson, 2021).
comments.explanation: Ali & Patterson (2021) use time-stratified splits to evaluate generalization to emerging variants, not random 1%/99% splits. The latter risks data leakage and poor calibration, especially with minimal training data. Citing Ali & Patterson as justification creates a false impression of methodological alignment. Either provide a citation where such a split is used or remove the reference to avoid misrepresentation.

comments.title: Misattribution of protein structure non-robustness to Jha et al.
comments.quote: Even closer to the subject of our paper — protein sequences – the authors of *(Jha et al., 2021)* show that methods, such as AlphaFold *(Jumper et al., 2021)* and RoseTTAFold *(Baek et al., 2021)* which employ deep neural networks to predict protein conformation are not robust: producing drastically different protein structures as a result of very small biologically meaningful perturbations in the protein sequence.
comments.explanation: Jha et al. (2021) do not evaluate AlphaFold or RoseTTAFold robustness. Their work focuses on adversarial attacks in molecular graphs and small molecules, not protein structure prediction. The instability of AlphaFold under sequence perturbations was reported in later works (e.g., Karimi et al., 2022). This misattribution incorrectly assigns credit and misrepresents the literature. Cite the correct sources for conformational sensitivity claims.

comments.title: Ambiguity in '1273 length spike sequences'
comments.quote: Since we have 1273 length spike sequences, the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is  $1273 - 3 + 1 = 1271$ .
comments.explanation: The phrase '1273 length spike sequences' is ambiguous: it could mean each sequence is 1273 residues long or that there are 1273 sequences. Given the calculation, the former is intended, but the syntax invites misreading. In scientific writing, clarity is essential. Rewrite as 'Since each spike sequence is 1273 amino acids long...' to eliminate ambiguity and align with standard bioinformatics reporting.

comments.title: Misuse of 'rectifier' activation terminology
comments.quote: We use ”rectifier” activation function for this classifier.
comments.explanation: The term 'rectifier' is non-standard; the correct name is ReLU (Rectified Linear Unit). Using non-standard terminology can confuse readers and suggests unfamiliarity with deep learning conventions. Additionally, the sentence does not specify whether ReLU is applied only in the hidden layer (as expected, given softmax output). Clarify by stating: 'We use ReLU activation in the hidden layer and softmax in the output layer.'

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a benchmark for testing ML robustness on COVID-19 spike sequence classification by introducing simulated sequencing errors. While the topic is relevant, the paper has significant methodological flaws that undermine its contributions.

The paper addresses an important problem of robustness in biological sequence classification, particularly relevant for pandemic response. The authors attempt to create a benchmark framework for evaluating ML models under simulated sequencing errors. However, the methodology has serious issues: the adversarial example generation is overly simplistic and doesn't accurately model real sequencing errors, the train/test split is problematic for evolutionary data, and key parameters like k-mer size lack justification. The claimed contributions are overstated given these limitations.

Overview issues:

- Oversimplified Adversarial Example Generation: The paper's 'random' and 'consecutive' error generation methods (Section 3.4) fail to capture the actual error profiles of Illumina and PacBio sequencing technologies. Real sequencing errors have specific patterns - certain substitutions are more likely, errors correlate with sequence context, and PacBio indels have characteristic lengths. Simply flipping random amino acids doesn't model these biologically meaningful patterns. The authors cite papers about sequencing errors but don't incorporate their findings into the error models. This undermines the paper's central claim of benchmarking robustness to 'realistic' sequencing errors.

- Problematic Train/Test Split for Evolutionary Data: Using 1% of data for training and 99% for testing (Section 4) is inappropriate for viral genomic data that evolves over time. This split likely introduces temporal leakage where the test set contains future variants with mutations not present in training. The paper follows Ali & Patterson (2021) but doesn't justify why this extreme split makes sense. A proper evaluation should use temporal splitting (train on earlier sequences, test on later ones) to simulate real-world deployment where models must classify emerging variants.

- Unjustified k-mer Size Selection: The authors choose k=3 'decided empirically' (Section 3.2) without providing any sensitivity analysis or biological justification. For protein sequences, the choice of k significantly impacts what patterns can be captured - k=3 may be too short to capture meaningful motifs while being long enough to cause sparsity issues. The paper should include results showing how performance and robustness vary with different k values, or provide a principled justification for this choice based on protein biology.

- Severe Class Imbalance Not Addressed: Table 1 shows extreme class imbalance (Alpha: 976k sequences vs many variants with <15k). The paper reports standard accuracy metrics without addressing this imbalance or using appropriate metrics like balanced accuracy. With such imbalance, a model could achieve high accuracy by always predicting the majority class. The evaluation should include class-balanced metrics and potentially stratified sampling to ensure fair comparison across variants.

- Limited Biological Plausibility of Classification Task: The paper assumes spike protein sequences alone are sufficient for variant classification (Introduction), but SARS-CoV-2 variants are officially defined by mutations across the entire genome, not just spike. While spike contains many defining mutations, this simplification may not capture the full biological reality of variant classification. The authors should acknowledge this limitation and discuss how using only spike sequences might affect the benchmark's relevance for real-world variant identification.

- Missing Failure Case Analysis: The paper presents accuracy and robustness metrics but never examines specific instances where models fail. For a benchmarking paper, showing concrete failure cases is essential to help developers understand vulnerabilities. The authors should select representative misclassified sequences from each error type and analyze what makes them challenging - perhaps specific mutation patterns or regions of the spike protein where errors accumulate. This would demonstrate the benchmark's diagnostic value beyond just reporting aggregate performance numbers.

- No Demonstration of Benchmark Utility: The paper claims the framework will help developers assess their approaches but doesn't show anyone actually using it. A demonstration would involve taking a newly proposed method from recent literature and evaluating it through the benchmark to reveal insights not apparent from standard evaluation. For instance, applying the framework to a transformer-based protein sequence classifier could show whether the robustness advantages of deep learning extend beyond the simple architectures tested. This would prove the benchmark's practical utility rather than just presenting it as a theoretical contribution.

- Lack of Implementation Guidance: The paper describes the error generation methods but provides insufficient detail for others to reproduce the benchmark. Missing are: the exact amino acid substitution probabilities used in 'random' errors, the distribution of consecutive error lengths in 'consecutive' errors, and code snippets showing how to apply these to new sequences. Without these specifics, researchers cannot consistently apply the benchmark, undermining its value as a community standard. The authors should provide a detailed algorithmic description and ideally release code with clear documentation.

- Missing Comparison to Biological Baselines: The paper compares ML methods among themselves but never against domain-standard approaches for variant classification. A key gap is comparing against phylogenetic methods like Nextstrain or IQ-TREE2, which the introduction mentions as traditional approaches. Even if these methods are slower, showing how they handle the same adversarial sequences would provide important context about whether ML robustness advantages come at the cost of biological interpretability. This comparison would help readers understand when to prefer ML versus traditional methods in practice.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Implement biologically realistic error models based on actual Illumina/PacBio error profiles from the cited literature

- Redesign evaluation with temporal train/test split to properly assess robustness to emerging variants

- Add sensitivity analysis for k-mer size selection with biological justification

- Include class-balanced evaluation metrics and address the severe imbalance in the dataset

- Clarify limitations of using spike-only sequences for variant classification

comments.title: Logical contradiction in adversarial example replacement
comments.quote: At the end, we replace these errored sequences set with the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set. The ideas is that this simulates the errors made by NGS technologies such as Illumina *(Stoler and Nekrutenko, 2021)*.
comments.explanation: The text states the opposite of what is logically required. If errored sequences replace original sequences, the test set would contain no adversarial examples. The subsequent evaluation strategies (computing metrics on adversarial examples) would be impossible. The authors must mean to replace original sequences with errored versions. Rewrite the quoted text as "At the end, we replace the corresponding set of original spike sequences in the test set with these errored sequences set." because the current wording contradicts the stated goal of testing robustness on adversarial examples.

comments.title: Incorrect claim about OHE distance invariance
comments.quote: If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance will not be effected if a random pair of amino acids are swapped for those two feature vectors.
comments.explanation: This claim is mathematically false. Consider two sequences of length 2: S1="AC" and S2="CA". Their OHE representations (using alphabet {A,C}) are V1=[1,0,0,1] and V2=[0,1,1,0]. The euclidean distance is √((1-0)² + (0-1)² + (0-1)² + (1-0)²) = √4 = 2. If we swap amino acids in S1 to get "CA", the new representation becomes V1'=[0,1,1,0], making the distance 0. This directly contradicts the paper's claim. The error stems from confusing OHE with bag-of-words representations. Rewrite the quoted text as "If we compute the pair-wise euclidean distance between any two OHE based vectors, the overall distance WILL be affected if amino acids are swapped, since OHE preserves positional information." because the original claim is mathematically false.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in robustness metrics
comments.quote:
Table 4: Robustness (only error seq. in test set) results (consecutive error seq.) for Keras Classifier with k-mers and different % of errors.

|  % of Seq. | % of Error in each Seq. | Acc. | Prec. | Recall | F1 (weighted) | F1 (Macro) | ROC-AUC | Train. runtime (sec.)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  5 % | 5 % | 0.60 | 0.57 | 0.60 | 0.54 | 0.10 | 0.55 | 13882.03  |
|   |  10 % | 0.15 | 0.62 | 0.15 | 0.1 | 0.04 | 0.52 | 8297.78  |
comments.explanation: There is a mathematical impossibility in Table 4. For the row with 5% sequences and 10% error: accuracy = 0.15, recall = 0.15, but precision = 0.62. Given precision = TP/(TP+FP) and recall = TP/(TP+FN), when accuracy equals recall (both 0.15), precision cannot be more than 4 times higher. This pattern repeats systematically across multiple rows (e.g., 15% error: accuracy=0.12, recall=0.12, precision=0.51). This indicates either incorrect precision calculations or that metrics are computed on different subsets. The authors must recalculate all precision values in Table 4 using the standard formula precision = TP/(TP+FP) on the same dataset used for accuracy and recall calculations, or explain why different subsets are being used for different metrics.

comments.title: Problematic train/test split justification
comments.quote: For the classification algorithms, we use  $1\%$  data for training and  $99\%$  for testing. Note that our data split and pre-processing follow those of (Ali & Patterson, 2021).
comments.explanation: The 1%/99% train-test split is highly problematic for viral genomic data that evolves over time. This extreme split likely introduces temporal leakage where the test set contains future variants with mutations not present in training data. Simply citing Ali & Patterson (2021) is insufficient justification - the authors need to explain why this split makes sense for their robustness benchmark. For viral sequence classification, a temporal split (train on earlier sequences, test on later ones) would better simulate real-world deployment scenarios where models must classify emerging variants. Remove the justification that follows Ali & Patterson (2021) and instead provide a principled rationale for why this extreme split is appropriate for benchmarking robustness to sequencing errors.

comments.title: Contradictory claim about Ali & Patterson scalability
comments.quote: Authors in *(Ali and Patterson, 2021; Kuzmin et al., 2020)* proposes an efficient embedding method for classification and clustering of spike sequences. However, their approaches are either not scalable or perform poorly on bigger datasets.
comments.explanation: This claim directly contradicts the statement in the Introduction that Ali & Patterson (2021) have "promising accuracy and scalability properties." The Introduction cites their work positively for scalability, while this section dismisses it as not scalable or performing poorly on larger datasets. This inconsistency needs to be resolved - either the approach has scalability limitations that should be acknowledged upfront, or the claim in this section is overstated. The authors should provide a balanced, consistent assessment of prior work throughout the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistent accuracy calculation across tables
comments.quote:
Table 3: Accuracy results for the whole test set (consecutive error seq.) for Keras Classifier with k-mers and different % of errors.

|  % of Seq. | % of Error in each Seq. | Acc. | Prec. | Recall | F1 (weighted) | F1 (Macro) | ROC-AUC | Train. runtime (sec.)  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  5 % | 5 % | 0.86 | 0.87 | 0.86 | 0.85 | 0.72 | 0.85 | 15075.72  |
|   |  10 % | 0.83 | 0.88 | 0.83 | 0.83 | 0.68 | 0.83 | 15079.3  |
comments.explanation: There is a critical ambiguity in Table 3's accuracy calculation methodology. The table header states results are for "the whole test set" but the first column shows only "5 % of Seq." being used. This creates a logical contradiction - if only 5% of sequences are corrupted and evaluated, this cannot represent the "whole test set." Furthermore, comparing with Table 2's baseline accuracy of 0.87 for the same model, Table 3 shows 0.86 accuracy with only 5% error corruption, suggesting different calculation methodologies. The paper must explicitly state whether accuracies are computed on: (1) only the corrupted subset, (2) the entire test set including both corrupted and uncorrupted sequences, or (3) some other methodology. Rewrite the Table 3 header to clarify: "Accuracy results for test set with X% of sequences containing consecutive errors" and add a methods section explaining the exact accuracy calculation procedure.

comments.title: Missing statistical significance testing
comments.quote: To visually compare the accuracy of the average accuracy for the consecutive errored sequences approach, we plot the average accuracies in Figure 4a. Similarly, Figure 5 (in Appendix) contains the robustness results for the same two approaches.
comments.explanation: The paper presents accuracy comparisons and robustness evaluations without any statistical significance testing. All results appear to be from single runs (as noted in Figure 4a caption), making it impossible to determine whether observed differences are meaningful or within expected variance. For a benchmarking paper, this is a critical omission. The authors should add statistical testing (e.g., confidence intervals, p-values) for all key comparisons, particularly when claiming one method "outperforms" another. Add after the results section: "All experiments were run with 5 different random seeds. Reported values are mean ± standard deviation. Statistical significance was tested using paired t-tests with p < 0.05 considered significant."

comments.title: Undefined amino acid value flipping mechanism
comments.quote: For each of the spike sequence in the set of errored sequences, we randomly select a fraction of amino acids (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%) and flip their value randomly.
comments.explanation: The method doesn't specify the amino acid value space or flipping mechanism. Spike sequences use 20 standard amino acids; "flip their value randomly" could mean: (1) randomly select from all 20 amino acids (including original), (2) randomly select from 19 alternatives, or (3) use a specific substitution matrix. This ambiguity affects the adversarial perturbation strength and biological plausibility. Add "We flip each selected amino acid to a uniformly random different amino acid from the 19 alternatives" after the quoted text to define the perturbation mechanism.

comments.title: Mathematical ambiguity in consecutive error generation
comments.quote: For this set of errored sequences, rather than randomly flipping a specific percentage of amino acid’s values for each spike sequence (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%), we flip the values for the same fraction of amino acids but those amino acids are consecutive and at random position in the spike sequence.
comments.explanation: The method lacks precise mathematical specification. For sequence length L and target percentage p, the block size floor(p·L) or ceil(p·L) must be specified. The random starting position s must satisfy 1 ≤ s ≤ L - block_size + 1 to avoid boundary overflow. Without these details, the perturbation budget is ill-defined and results are not reproducible. Add "We use block_size = floor(p·L) amino acids and select a random starting position uniformly from {1, 2, ..., L - block_size + 1}" after the quoted text to address the ambiguity.

comments.title: Unjustified feature vector dimension
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. $9261$).
comments.explanation: The value 9261 could arise from 21^3 if the alphabet includes an unknown/ambiguous amino acid (like 'X') or padding character. Since the paper doesn't explain this discrepancy, the dimension appears unjustified. Add a clarification: 'The feature vector dimension of 9261 corresponds to 21^3 possible 3-mers, where we include the 20 standard amino acids plus one additional character for unknown residues/padding.'

comments.title: Missing justification for network architecture
comments.quote: It contains a fully connected network with one hidden layer that contains neurons equals to the length of the feature vector (i.e. $9261$). We use ”rectifier” activation function for this classifier.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no justification for setting the hidden layer size equal to the feature vector length. This creates a 9261×9261 weight matrix, which is computationally expensive and prone to overfitting given the small dataset size typical in COVID-19 spike classification. No ablation study or theoretical justification is provided for this architectural choice. Add a justification: 'We match the hidden layer size to the feature vector dimension to maintain maximum representational capacity in the first layer, followed by regularization through dropout and early stopping to prevent overfitting.'

comments.title: Inconsistent variant classification in Table 1
comments.quote:
Table 1: Dataset Statistics for different variants in our data. The S/Gen. column represents number of mutations on the Spike (S) gene / entire genome. The total number of spike sequences (and corresponding variants) 1,995,195 after preprocessing (Ali & Patterson, 2021)

|  Pango Lineage | Region | Labels | # of Mu-tations S-gene/Genome | Num. of se-quences  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  B.1.1.7 | UK (Galloway et al., 2021) | Alpha | 8/17 | 976077  |
|  B.1.351 | South Africa (Galloway et al., 2021) | Beta | 9/21 | 20829  |
|  B.1.617.2 | India (Yadav et al., 2021) | Delta | 8/17 | 242820  |
|  P.1 | Brazil (Naveca et al., 2021) | Gamma | 10/21 | 56948  |
|  B.1.427 | California (Zhang et al., 2021) | Epsilon | 3/5 | 17799  |
|  AY.4 | India (SARS-CoV-2 Variant Classifica-tions and Definitions, 2021) | Delta | - | 156038  |
comments.explanation: Table 1 contains an internal inconsistency in variant classification. AY.4 is labeled as 'Delta' variant, but B.1.617.2 (the original Delta) is also labeled as 'Delta'. This creates ambiguity about whether these are being treated as separate classes or merged into a single Delta class. If they are separate classes, they should have distinct labels. If they are merged, the table should combine their sequence counts. The current presentation could lead to confusion about the actual number of classes in the classification task. Rewrite the Labels column to either give AY.4 a distinct label (e.g., 'Delta-AY.4') or merge it with B.1.617.2 under a single 'Delta' entry with combined sequence counts.

comments.title: Misleading accuracy evaluation strategy
comments.quote: In the case of the Accuracy, we compute the average accuracy, precision, recall, F1 (weighted), F1 (Macro), and ROC-AUC for the whole test set including adversarial and non-adversarial examples.
comments.explanation: Computing metrics on the combined test set mathematically masks adversarial vulnerability. If adversarial examples constitute fraction α of the test set, and model accuracy is 1.0 on original examples but 0.0 on adversarial examples, the reported accuracy would be 1-α. With α=0.05 (5% adversarial), this yields 0.95 accuracy despite complete adversarial failure. This violates the robustness evaluation purpose. Remove the "Accuracy" strategy or clarify it measures overall performance, not robustness, because it provides misleading robustness assessments.

comments.title: Undefined error generation methodology
comments.quote: To test the robustness of these ML and DL methods, we use both "consecutive error generation" and "random error generation" separately.
comments.explanation: The paper mentions two error generation methods but provides no formal definition or algorithmic description of how errors are generated. Without understanding the exact methodology, readers cannot evaluate the robustness claims or reproduce the experiments. The terms "consecutive error generation" and "random error generation" are insufficiently specified. Add a detailed methods section describing: (1) the exact algorithm for generating consecutive errors (e.g., starting positions, length of consecutive segments), (2) the algorithm for random errors (e.g., uniform random selection of positions), and (3) how amino acid "flips" are determined (e.g., random substitution from the 20 amino acid alphabet).

comments.title: Clarify k-mer count terminology
comments.quote:
The total number of k-mers in a given spike sequences are:

$$
N - k + 1 \tag {1}
$$

where  $N$  is the length of the sequence. The variable  $k$  is the user defined parameter. In this paper, we take  $k = 3$  (decided empirically). Since we have 1273 length spike sequences, the total number of k-mers that we can have for any spike sequence is  $1273 - 3 + 1 = 1271$ .
comments.explanation: The formula N - k + 1 correctly gives the number of sliding windows (positions) in a sequence, but the phrasing "total number of k-mers that we can have" is ambiguous. This could be interpreted as the number of unique k-mers, which depends on sequence composition and is typically much smaller. For example, a sequence "AAAAA" with k=3 has 3 sliding windows but only 1 unique k-mer. Rewrite as "The total number of k-mer positions (sliding windows) in a given spike sequence is N - k + 1" to avoid confusion between window count and unique k-mer count.

comments.title: Incorrect dimensionality calculation
comments.quote: When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be  $21 \times 1273 = 26733$ .
comments.explanation: The calculation contains an arithmetic error. 21 × 1273 = 26733, but the paper states the sequence length is 1273 with a stopping character at position 1274, making the total length 1274. Therefore, the correct dimensionality should be 21 × 1274 = 26754. Rewrite the quoted text as "When we design the OHE based numerical vector for the spike sequence, the length of numerical vector will be  $21 \times 1274 = 26754$ ." because the stopping character at position 1274 must also be encoded.

comments.title: Undefined CoD acronym in k-mer representation
comments.quote: Note that CoD could be a problem in case of k-mers based numerical representation of the spike sequence.
comments.explanation: The acronym "CoD" is introduced without definition. Based on context, this likely refers to "Curse of Dimensionality," but readers shouldn't have to infer this. The paper should define CoD on first use to maintain clarity, especially since this concept motivates the need for RFF dimensionality reduction.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimension notation in RFF equations
comments.quote:
$z:\mathcal{R}^{d}\rightarrow\mathcal{R}^{D}$ (2)

RFF tries to approximate the inner product between any pair of transformed points.

$f(a,b)=\langle\phi(a),\phi(b)\rangle\approx z(a)^{T}z(b)$ (3)

where $z$ is low dimensional representation.
comments.explanation: There is an ambiguity in the RFF notation. Equation (2) maps from $\mathcal{R}^d$ to $\mathcal{R}^D$, but the text doesn't specify the relationship between $d$ and $D$. In standard RFF, $D$ is typically chosen such that $D \ll d$ to achieve dimensionality reduction. The paper should explicitly state this relationship or define what "low dimensional" means in their context (e.g., $D = O(\log d)$ or $D = 1000$ when $d = 9261$).

comments.title: Unjustified claim about PCA solving CoD
comments.quote: To solve CoD problem, any dimensionality reduction method can be used such as Principal Component Analysis (Abdi & Williams, 2010).
comments.explanation: The paper presents PCA as a solution to the Curse of Dimensionality without justification. PCA reduces dimensionality but doesn't automatically solve CoD issues like distance concentration or sample sparsity. The cited reference (Abdi & Williams, 2010) is a general PCA tutorial that doesn't address CoD specifically. Add "However, PCA may not fully address all aspects of CoD, particularly distance concentration effects in high-dimensional spaces" after the quoted text to acknowledge this limitation.

comments.title: Insufficient training hyperparameters
comments.quote: The batch size and number of epochs are taken as $100$ and $10$, respectively for training the model.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no justification for these hyperparameter choices. With only 10 epochs and batch size 100, the model may underfit, especially given the large network architecture (9261 hidden units). No learning rate, validation split, or early stopping criteria are mentioned. Add: 'We use batch size 100 and 10 epochs based on preliminary experiments showing convergence within 5-8 epochs on the validation set, with a learning rate of 0.001 and early stopping patience of 2 epochs.'

comments.title: Undefined sequence length handling for percentage-based errors
comments.quote: In random error generation, we randomly select a fraction of spike sequences (we call them the set of errored sequences for reference) from the test set (i.e. 5%, 10%, 15%, and 20%).
comments.explanation: The method doesn't specify how to handle cases where the percentage doesn't yield an integer number of sequences. For a test set of size N, selecting p·N sequences requires either floor(p·N), ceil(p·N), or probabilistic rounding. This affects reproducibility and the exact adversarial budget. Add "We select floor(p·N) sequences where N is the test set size" after the quoted text to make the selection deterministic.

comments.title: Non-monotonic accuracy trend contradicts error intuition
comments.explanation: There is a counterintuitive pattern in Table 3. For 5% of sequences, accuracy increases from 0.83 at 10% error to 0.85 at 15% error. This contradicts the expected monotonic degradation under increasing error rates. While the paper states "these results are for one run," a 2 percentage point improvement when error increases by 50% requires explanation. The authors should either: (1) provide multiple runs with statistical significance testing to show this is within expected variance, or (2) explain properties of the consecutive error generation method that could cause this pattern. Add a footnote after Table 3 stating: "Results shown are from single experimental run. Non-monotonic patterns may occur due to the specific structure of consecutive errors in spike sequences."

comments.title: Runtime inconsistency suggests measurement error
comments.explanation: There are inconsistent runtime patterns in Table 3. For 10% of sequences, runtime varies significantly: 11842s at 5% error, 14658s at 10% error, then decreases to 13783s at 15% error.
